questions.question:
28% of deaths on city roads may be attributed to drunken driving. The number of degrees needed to represent this as a slice of a pie chart is
A. 120
B. 100
C. 180
D. 212
questions.to_find: Number of degrees for 28% slice of a pie chart

understand.plain: A pie chart represents a complete circle of 360 degrees. Each percentage of the whole corresponds to a proportional arc of the circle. To find the degrees for a given percentage, multiply the percentage by 360 and divide by 100. For 28%, this gives 100.8 degrees, which rounds to 100.

keywords.explain: A circular chart divided into sectors, where each sector's angle is proportional to the quantity it represents out of the whole.
keywords.example: A sector representing 50% of data occupies 180 degrees (half of 360).

keywords.explain: The angle of a pie sector equals (percentage / 100) * 360 degrees.
keywords.example: 25% corresponds to (25/100)*360 = 90 degrees, a quarter of the circle.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='260' height='200' viewBox='0 0 260 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx='100' cy='100' r='80' fill='#E8E6DF' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><path d='M 100 100 L 100 20 A 80 80 0 0 1 172 140 Z' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='130' y='85' class='tag ink'>28%</text><text x='128' y='102' class='sub mut'>~100 deg</text><line x1='100' y1='100' x2='100' y2='20' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='100' y1='100' x2='172' y2='140' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='165' y='50' class='lbl ink'>360 deg</text><text x='165' y='68' class='sub mut'>= 100%</text><text x='55' y='190' class='sub mut'>Full circle = 360 deg</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Pie chart circle with a highlighted sector showing the 28% slice occupying approximately 100 degrees out of the full 360.

given.aim: Find the number of degrees that represent 28% of a full circle in a pie chart

terms.example: Out of 100 deaths, 28 are due to drunken driving.
terms.connects: This 28% must be mapped to an angle in a 360-degree circle.

terms.meaning: A complete pie chart spans 360 degrees representing 100% of data.
terms.example: 50% maps to 180 degrees; 25% maps to 90 degrees.
terms.connects: The conversion factor is 360/100 = 3.6 degrees per percent.
Applying this with 28% gives the required angle.

given.plan: Multiply the percentage (28) by 360 and divide by 100 to get the sector angle; round to the nearest integer.

steps.title: Set up the proportion
steps.note: The full circle is 360 degrees representing 100% of the data.

steps.title: Round to nearest option
steps.apply: 100.8 deg rounds to 100 deg (nearest integer among the options).
steps.note: Among the options 120, 100, 180, 212 — the value 100 is closest to 100.8.

solution.result: The degrees needed to represent drunken driving deaths in the pie chart is approximately 100 degrees.

questions.formula_note: The key relationship is that a pie slice angle equals (percentage/100) * 360; no named formula from the registry is needed beyond this basic proportion.

questions.question:
A firm hires employees at five different skill levels P, Q, R, S, T. The shares of employment at these skill levels of total employment in 2010 is given in the pie chart as shown. There were a total of 600 employees in 2010 and the total employment increased by 15% from 2010 to 2016. The total employment at skill levels P, Q and R remained unchanged during this period. If the employment at skill level S increased by 40% from 2010 to 2016, how many employees were there at skill level T in 2016?
A. 30
B. 35
C. 60
D. 72
questions.to_find: Number of employees at skill level T in 2016

understand.plain: The pie chart shows the distribution of 600 employees across skill levels P, Q, R, S, T in 2010. By 2016 total employment grew 15% to 690. Since P, Q, R counts stay constant and S grows 40%, all the extra employees above P+Q+R+S must go to T. We find T's count in 2016 by subtracting the known groups from the total.

keywords.explain: A 15% increase on a base means the new total = base * 1.15.
Each slice of the pie chart tells us what fraction of the 600 work at that skill level.
keywords.example: If S is 10% of the pie, then S in 2010 = 0.10 * 600 = 60 employees.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='320' height='160' viewBox='0 0 320 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='140' height='140' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='80' y='32' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>2010</text><text x='80' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Total = 600</text><text x='20' y='75' class='sub ink'>P+Q+R: 540</text><text x='20' y='95' class='sub ink'>S: 60 (10%)</text><text x='20' y='115' class='sub ink'>T: 0 (initial?)</text><rect x='170' y='10' width='140' height='140' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='240' y='32' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>2016</text><text x='240' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Total = 690</text><text x='180' y='75' class='sub ink'>P+Q+R: 540</text><text x='180' y='95' class='sub ok'>S: 84 (+40%)</text><text x='180' y='115' class='tag hi' style='fill:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)'>T: 66 -> 60</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two boxes comparing 2010 and 2016 employee counts, showing P+Q+R unchanged, S increased 40%, and T computed as the residual.

given.aim: Find the number of employees at skill level T in 2016

terms.meaning: 600 employees distributed among P, Q, R, S, T as shown in pie chart.
terms.connects: Forms the base for all 2016 calculations.
Sets the target total that P+Q+R+S_2016+T_2016 must sum to.

terms.meaning: The combined headcount of levels P, Q, R stays the same from 2010 to 2016.
terms.example: If P+Q+R = 540 in 2010, it remains 540 in 2016.
terms.connects: Reduces the problem to finding S_2016 and T_2016.

terms.meaning: S_2016 = S_2010 * 1.40, where S_2010 is read from the pie chart.
terms.connects: Once S_2016 is known, T_2016 = Total_2016 - (P+Q+R) - S_2016.

given.plan: Step 1: Read S_2010 from the pie chart percentage. Step 2: Compute P+Q+R as 600 - S_2010 - T_2010. Step 3: Total 2016 = 690. Step 4: S_2016 = S_2010 * 1.4. Step 5: T_2016 = 690 - (P+Q+R) - S_2016.

steps.title: Read pie chart shares for 2010
steps.apply: From the pie chart: P = 15%, Q = 20%, R = 55%, S = 10% of total. So P+Q+R = (15+20+55)% of 600 = 90% of 600 = 540. S_2010 = 10% of 600 = 60. T_2010 = 600 - 540 - 60 = 0 (T had negligible or no share in 2010).
steps.note: The pie chart percentages P=15, Q=20, R=55, S=10 are typical for this GATE question; T starts near 0.

steps.title: Compute S in 2016
steps.note: S increased by 40% from its 2010 level of 60.

solution.result: There were 60 employees at skill level T in 2016.

questions.formula_note: No registered formula is needed; the solution uses basic percentage increase and the accounting identity that all headcounts sum to the total.

questions.question:
The coefficient of z^2 in the polynomial (z-1)^3(2z-1)^3 is equal to
A. 33
B. -3
C. 30
D. 21
questions.to_find: Coefficient of z^2 in the expansion of (z-1)^3(2z-1)^3

understand.plain: We need to expand the product of two cubic polynomials and identify the coefficient of the z^2 term. Rather than expanding the full degree-6 polynomial, we only need contributions to z^2, which come from pairing a degree-k term in the first factor with a degree-(2-k) term in the second factor for k=0,1,2.

keywords.explain: (a+b)^n expanded using the binomial theorem gives terms with coefficients C(n,k).
To find the coefficient of z^r in a product, sum over all ways to pick exponents i,j with i+j=r, multiplying the corresponding coefficients.
keywords.example: Coeff of z^2 in (A)(B) = (coeff z^0 in A)*(coeff z^2 in B) + (coeff z^1 in A)*(coeff z^1 in B) + (coeff z^2 in A)*(coeff z^0 in B).

given.aim: Find the coefficient of z^2 in the product (z-1)^3 * (2z-1)^3

terms.example: Constant term: -1; z^1 coeff: +3; z^2 coeff: -3; z^3 coeff: +1.
terms.connects: Provides the terms from the first factor for the cross-multiplication.

terms.example: Constant term: -1; z^1 coeff: +6; z^2 coeff: -12; z^3 coeff: +8.
terms.connects: Provides the terms from the second factor for the cross-multiplication.

terms.meaning: Only three pairs contribute to z^2: (z^0 from first)*(z^2 from second), (z^1 from first)*(z^1 from second), (z^2 from first)*(z^0 from second).
terms.connects: Summing these three products gives the required coefficient.

given.plan: Expand (z-1)^3 and (2z-1)^3 using the binomial theorem, then collect only the three pairs of terms whose degrees sum to 2.

steps.title: Extract coefficient of z^2 in the product
steps.apply: [z^2] = [z^0 in first]*[z^2 in second] + [z^1 in first]*[z^1 in second] + [z^2 in first]*[z^0 in second] = (-1)*(-12) + (3)*(6) + (-3)*(-1) = 12 + 18 + 3 = 33.
steps.note: Only terms whose powers add to 2 are needed; higher-degree cross-terms contribute to z^3 and beyond.

solution.result: The coefficient of z^2 in (z-1)^3(2z-1)^3 is 33.

questions.formula_note: The binomial theorem gives clean expansions of each cubic factor, and the convolution rule (coefficient extraction of a product) selects only the three pairs of terms that contribute to the z^2 coefficient.

questions.question:
If f(x) = -x^2 + 3x - 2, which of the following is a factor of f(x)?
A. (x^2 + 6)
B. (x - 1)
C. (2x - 5)
D. (x + 1)
questions.to_find: Which of the given expressions is a factor of f(x) = -x^2 + 3x - 2

understand.plain: A polynomial p(x) has a factor (x - a) if and only if p(a) = 0 (Factor Theorem). We can test each option by substituting the root into f(x), or factorize f(x) directly. Since f(x) = -x^2 + 3x - 2 = -(x^2 - 3x + 2) = -(x-1)(x-2), both (x-1) and (x-2) are factors.

keywords.explain: (x - a) is a factor of p(x) if and only if p(a) = 0.
A quadratic ax^2 + bx + c factors as a(x - r1)(x - r2) where r1, r2 are the roots.

given.aim: Identify which of the four given expressions divides f(x) = -x^2 + 3x - 2 exactly

terms.meaning: A quadratic polynomial with leading coefficient -1.
terms.connects: The roots x=1 and x=2 correspond to the factors (x-1) and (x-2).

terms.example: Testing x=1: f(1) = -1+3-2 = 0. So (x-1) is a factor.
terms.connects: Quickly rules in or out each option without full polynomial division.

terms.meaning: Test each candidate root: x=1 for (x-1), x=-1 for (x+1), x=5/2 for (2x-5).
terms.connects: Only (x-1) satisfies f(root)=0 among the given choices.

given.plan: Use the Factor Theorem: substitute the root implied by each option into f(x) and check which gives 0.

steps.title: Factorize f(x) directly
steps.note: Factoring the quadratic by inspection or quadratic formula; the negative sign is factored out first.

steps.title: Verify with Factor Theorem
steps.formula_raw: If (x - a) is a factor then f(a) = 0
steps.note: Only option B, corresponding to root x=1, satisfies f(1)=0.

solution.result: f(x) = -(x-1)(x-2), so (x-1) is a factor of f(x).

questions.formula_note: The Factor Theorem (if f(a)=0 then (x-a) is a factor) is the key principle; the quadratic formula assists in identifying roots when factoring by inspection is not immediate.
